content.text: Production AI agents differ fundamentally from conventional web services: they are long-running, autonomous, and spend real money on every step. A single runaway agent can exhaust a token budget in minutes, enter an infinite retry loop that fills a message queue, or silently produce degraded output for hours before anyone notices. Alerting for agents is the practice of defining threshold-based rules on observed signals — token cost, latency, error rate, tool call depth, loop count — and automatically routing notifications or triggering remediation when those thresholds are crossed.

Unlike static web servers, agents exhibit emergent failure modes: they may get stuck waiting for a tool that never responds, hallucinate a tool call schema that causes every invocation to fail, or successively escalate their own context window until they hit the model's limit. These failures do not produce 500-class HTTP errors visible to a load balancer. They must be caught by instrumentation woven into the agent runtime itself, exposed as time-series metrics, and evaluated by an alert rules engine that understands what "normal" looks like for an autonomous reasoning loop.

The outputs of the alerting system range from a Slack notification warning an engineer that cost is at 80% of budget, to a PagerDuty page waking an on-call responder when the error rate crosses 5%, to a fully automated kill switch that stops new agent runs until a circuit breaker is reset. Alerting sits at the intersection of observability (knowing what is happening) and operations (responding to it).

The financial stakes of unmonitored agent deployments are higher than in most software systems. An LLM call costs real money — at scale, even a 10% regression in token efficiency compounds into hundreds of dollars per day. An agent fleet running one thousand tasks per hour at an average of $0.03 each generates $30/hr. A bug that doubles token usage goes undetected without alerting and doubles the cost silently. The first sign of the problem might be a bill at the end of the month.

Quality degradation is equally invisible without signals. An agent that started producing shorter, lower-quality outputs after a model version upgrade will not raise an exception. It will simply generate outputs that fail downstream validation or that users eventually report as unhelpful. Without latency percentiles, error rates, and output quality signals wired into alerts, the regression lives in production for hours or days before it is caught.

Reliability failures — stuck agents, retry storms, and infinite loops — are the most acute risk. An agent loop that retries indefinitely on a transient API error will consume CPU, tokens, and money until it is manually killed. A stuck agent waiting on a deadlocked tool call blocks a task queue slot, potentially causing a cascading throughput collapse across the entire fleet. Alerting converts these invisible failure modes into actionable signals before they become outages.

Token spend alerts with graduated thresholds (warn at 70%, page at 90%, kill at 100%) prevent budget overruns before they appear on an invoice. Hourly and per-run cost metrics give fine-grained visibility into which agents or task types drive spend.

An alert on a rising error rate catches a newly deployed prompt regression within minutes. Without alerting, the same regression might run for hours before a user report or manual log review surfaces it.

Alerts on loop count per run and total tool call depth catch agents that have entered pathological retry or reasoning spirals. A dead man's switch — an alert that fires if a run has not completed within a wall-clock budget — catches stuck agents.
P95 latency alerts ensure that tail-latency regressions violating user-facing SLAs are caught and escalated before the SLA window expires.
Sustained high-water-mark alerts on queue depth and concurrent runs surface saturation before it causes dropped tasks, giving operators time to scale horizontally.
Alert history provides a timestamped record of anomalies, invaluable for post-incident reviews and for demonstrating compliance with safety or reliability commitments.
Critical alerts can trigger automated responses — killing stuck runs, enabling a circuit breaker, reducing concurrency — without requiring a human in the loop for well-understood failure modes.

Alert fatigue is the primary operational hazard. An alert system that pages for every minor cost fluctuation trains operators to ignore pages, which is worse than no alerting at all. Setting thresholds requires historical data on what "normal" looks like for each agent type — a threshold appropriate for a batch research pipeline is far too permissive for a real-time customer-facing agent.

Instrumentation overhead is real but manageable. Wrapping every LLM call and tool invocation with metrics collection adds a few microseconds of in-process latency and a small amount of network overhead for metric writes. In a high-frequency, sub-second agent loop this may be visible; for the typical second-to-minute agent step it is negligible.

Token cost spikes caused by intentionally large tasks (a long document to summarize) will fire cost alerts unless thresholds are set per task type or dynamically. Per-agent-type label cardinality must be managed carefully.

As models improve or prompts change, normal token usage shifts. Thresholds set months ago silently become incorrect — either too tight (causing alert fatigue) or too loose (missing real regressions). Regular threshold review is mandatory.

In multi-agent systems, a single user request fans out to sub-agents. Attributing cost and latency to the originating request rather than to individual sub-agent calls requires distributed tracing, which is significantly more complex than per-process metrics.

Cost and latency are cheap to measure. Output quality — does the agent actually answer the question correctly — requires an evaluation pipeline (an LLM judge or human rater), which is expensive to run in real time and adds significant complexity.
The alerting pipeline has four stages: instrumentation, collection, evaluation, and routing.

is the code that emits signals from within the agent. The agent SDK (or a wrapper around it) records a counter increment and a histogram observation on every LLM call, a gauge update when a tool call starts and finishes, and a span with start/end timestamps for the full agent run. Modern agentic frameworks (LangChain, LlamaIndex, custom agents) support callback hooks where this instrumentation can be injected without modifying core logic. The OpenTelemetry SDK provides a vendor-neutral format for metrics, traces, and logs.

is the infrastructure that receives and stores those signals. Prometheus scrapes a metrics endpoint exposed by each agent process every 15 seconds. An OTEL Collector receives spans via gRPC and forwards them to a tracing backend (Jaeger, Tempo). A log aggregator (Loki, CloudWatch Logs, Datadog Logs) receives structured JSON logs with run IDs and token counts.

is the alert rules engine. In Prometheus, rules are written in PromQL and evaluated on a configurable interval (typically 15–60 seconds). A rule specifies an expression (e.g., the 5-minute rate of errors divided by the rate of all requests), a threshold, and a duration for which the condition must be true before the alert fires. The duration filter prevents transient spikes from triggering pages.

is handled by Alertmanager (for Prometheus) or an equivalent routing layer. Alerts carry labels — severity, agent type, team owner — and the router matches those labels to receiver configurations: a Slack channel for warnings, a PagerDuty integration for critical pages, an auto-remediation webhook for well-understood failure patterns. Deduplication, grouping, and silencing are configured at the routing layer.

attrs.alt: Alerting for Agents architecture: Agent Runtime emits metrics via OTEL instrumentation to a Metrics Store (Prometheus/InfluxDB), which feeds an Alert Rules Engine evaluating cost, latency, and error thresholds. Firing alerts route to Notifications (PagerDuty, Slack, auto-remediation). An Observability Dashboard shows live stat tiles for token cost, p95 latency, success rate, and tool call depth, with an Active Alerts panel.

content.text: The diagram shows the four-stage pipeline. The
block on the left contains multiple agent instances instrumented via OpenTelemetry or a framework-specific callback. Raw signals flow right into the
(Prometheus or InfluxDB), which holds time-series data scraped every 15 seconds. The
continuously evaluates PromQL expressions against the store and fires alerts into
— PagerDuty for critical incidents, Slack for warnings, automated webhooks for self-healing. The

at the bottom reads the same store and presents live stat tiles alongside an Active Alerts panel. Dashed lines indicate that alert state from the rules engine and notification layer also feeds back into the dashboard for unified incident view.

Alerting is appropriate for any agent that runs in production with real users, real costs, or real SLAs. Even a lightly loaded agent handling twenty requests per hour warrants at minimum a dead man's switch (alert if no tasks complete in N minutes) and a cost alert (alert if spend exceeds budget). The investment in instrumentation is small; the cost of a silent failure is potentially large.
whenever token spend is material. Any agent making direct LLM API calls should have a per-hour or per-day cost counter with graduated thresholds.
whenever there is a user-facing or downstream SLA. Alert on the 95th percentile, not the mean — the mean hides the worst 5% of users who are experiencing timeouts.
for any agent that can fail silently (returning a degraded output or an empty result rather than an exception). A rising error rate signals prompt regression, model API issues, or tool failures.
for agents with autonomous reasoning loops. An agent taking 20+ reasoning steps when the normal maximum is 8 is almost certainly stuck or in an infinite retry spiral.
the agent is purely experimental, runs once-off, or has no budget sensitivity. Over-alerting experimental systems trains engineers to ignore alerts, which is counterproductive.
Do not alert on every individual run failure.

Individual task failures are expected and should be handled by retry logic. Alert on the rate of failures over a window — a 5% error rate sustained for two minutes is signal; a single failure is noise.

For each agent, enumerate the failure modes that would cause user impact or cost overrun. Map each to a measurable signal: token count per run, wall-clock duration, tool error count, number of reasoning iterations.
Add a metrics wrapper around LLM calls and tool calls using the Prometheus Python client (
) or the OpenTelemetry SDK. Record counters for call counts and errors, histograms for latency and token counts, and gauges for active run counts.
Start an HTTP server on a dedicated port (e.g., 9090) that serves the
endpoint in Prometheus exposition format. Add the agent's host and port to Prometheus's scrape configuration.
Define PromQL expressions for each signal. Use a
) to require the condition to persist before firing, avoiding transient false positives.
Set up receiver configurations for Slack (warnings) and PagerDuty (critical). Define grouping keys (e.g.,
) to collapse related alerts into a single notification during incident storms.

Import the metrics into Grafana. Create one panel per key signal with threshold bands overlaid (green below warn, yellow warn-to-critical, red above critical). Add the Alertmanager alert state panel so active alerts are visible alongside metric history.

Inject synthetic traffic that deliberately crosses each threshold. Verify the alert fires within the expected delay and that the notification reaches the intended channel. Silence the alert afterward and document the test in a runbook.

Examine the alert history. Thresholds generating more than two warning-level alerts per day with no resulting action are too tight. Thresholds that have never fired despite known incidents are too loose. Adjust iteratively.

# Agent instrumentation with prometheus_client
# pip install prometheus_client anthropic

from prometheus_client import Counter, Histogram, Gauge, start_http_server
import anthropic
import time

# --- Metric definitions ---
llm_calls_total = Counter(
    "agent_llm_calls_total",
    "Total number of LLM API calls made by the agent",
    ["agent_type", "model", "status"],  # labels
)
llm_tokens_total = Counter(
    "agent_llm_tokens_total",
    "Total tokens consumed (input + output)",
    ["agent_type", "direction"],  # direction: input / output
)
llm_latency_seconds = Histogram(
    "agent_llm_latency_seconds",
    "LLM call latency in seconds",
    ["agent_type", "model"],
    buckets=[0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 30, 60],
)
agent_runs_active = Gauge(
    "agent_runs_active",
    "Number of agent runs currently in progress",
    ["agent_type"],
)
agent_run_errors_total = Counter(
    "agent_run_errors_total",
    "Total number of agent run failures",
    ["agent_type", "error_class"],
)

# --- Instrumented LLM call wrapper ---
def call_llm_with_metrics(
    client: anthropic.Anthropic,
    messages: list[dict],
    agent_type: str = "research",
    model: str = "claude-sonnet-4-5",
    max_tokens: int = 1024,
) -> anthropic.types.Message:
    start = time.perf_counter()
    status = "success"
    try:
        response = client.messages.create(
            model=model,
            max_tokens=max_tokens,
            messages=messages,
        )
        llm_tokens_total.labels(agent_type=agent_type, direction="input").inc(
            response.usage.input_tokens
        )
        llm_tokens_total.labels(agent_type=agent_type, direction="output").inc(
            response.usage.output_tokens
        )
        return response
    except anthropic.RateLimitError:
        status = "rate_limit"
        raise
    except anthropic.APIError:
        status = "api_error"
        raise
    finally:
        elapsed = time.perf_counter() - start
        llm_calls_total.labels(
            agent_type=agent_type, model=model, status=status
        ).inc()
        llm_latency_seconds.labels(agent_type=agent_type, model=model).observe(elapsed)


# --- Agent run wrapper ---
def run_agent_with_metrics(task: str, agent_type: str = "research") -> str:
    agent_runs_active.labels(agent_type=agent_type).inc()
    try:
        client = anthropic.Anthropic()
        result = run_core_agent(client, task, agent_type)  # your agent logic
        return result
    except Exception as exc:
        agent_run_errors_total.labels(
            agent_type=agent_type, error_class=type(exc).__name__
        ).inc()
        raise
    finally:
        agent_runs_active.labels(agent_type=agent_type).dec()


# Expose metrics on port 9090 for Prometheus scraping
start_http_server(9090)

# prometheus/rules/agent_alerts.yaml
# Deploy to Prometheus rules directory; reload with: kill -HUP <prometheus_pid>

groups:
  - name: agent_cost
    interval: 60s   # evaluate every 60 seconds
    rules:
      - alert: AgentTokenCostWarning
        # Estimate cost: claude-sonnet ~$3/M input tokens, $15/M output tokens.
        # This approximation uses a blended rate of $6/M tokens.
        expr: |
          sum by (agent_type) (
            rate(agent_llm_tokens_total[1h])
          ) * 6 / 1e6 > 7
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "Agent {{ $labels.agent_type }} cost approaching budget"
          description: >-
            Estimated hourly token cost is ${{ $value | printf "%.2f" }},
            approaching the $10/hr budget cap.

      - alert: AgentTokenCostCritical
        expr: |
          sum by (agent_type) (
            rate(agent_llm_tokens_total[1h])
          ) * 6 / 1e6 > 10
        for: 2m
        labels:
          severity: critical
        annotations:
          summary: "Agent {{ $labels.agent_type }} budget exceeded"
          description: "Hourly cost ${{ $value | printf "%.2f" }} exceeds $10 cap. Kill switch may trigger."

  - name: agent_latency
    rules:
      - alert: AgentLatencyHigh
        expr: |
          histogram_quantile(0.95,
            rate(agent_llm_latency_seconds_bucket[5m])
          ) > 30
        for: 2m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "p95 LLM latency is {{ $value | printf "%.0f" }}s (threshold 30s)"

  - name: agent_errors
    rules:
      - alert: AgentErrorRateHigh
        expr: |
          sum by (agent_type) (
            rate(agent_run_errors_total[5m])
          )
          /
          sum by (agent_type) (
            rate(agent_llm_calls_total[5m])
          ) > 0.05
        for: 2m
        labels:
          severity: critical
        annotations:
          summary: "{{ $labels.agent_type }} error rate {{ $value | printf "%.1%" }}"

      - alert: AgentStuck
        # Fires if no LLM calls happen for 10 minutes while runs are active.
        # This is the "dead man's switch" for frozen agents.
        expr: |
          agent_runs_active > 0
          and
          rate(agent_llm_calls_total[10m]) == 0
        for: 10m
        labels:
          severity: critical
        annotations:
          summary: "Agent has {{ $labels.agent_type }} active runs but no LLM calls in 10m"

# alertmanager/alertmanager.yaml

global:
  resolve_timeout: 5m
  slack_api_url: "https://hooks.slack.com/services/YOUR/WEBHOOK"

route:
  receiver: slack-warnings
  group_by: [alertname, agent_type]
  group_wait: 30s        # wait before sending the first notification
  group_interval: 5m     # minimum interval between grouped notifications
  repeat_interval: 4h    # re-notify if still firing after 4 hours
  routes:
    - match:
        severity: critical
      receiver: pagerduty-critical
      continue: true      # also send to Slack
    - match:
        severity: critical
        alertname: AgentTokenCostCritical
      receiver: auto-kill-switch

receivers:
  - name: slack-warnings
    slack_configs:
      - channel: "#agent-alerts"
        title: "[{{ .Status | toUpper }}] {{ .CommonLabels.alertname }}"
        text: "{{ range .Alerts }}{{ .Annotations.description }}\n{{ end }}"
        send_resolved: true

  - name: pagerduty-critical
    pagerduty_configs:
      - routing_key: "YOUR_PAGERDUTY_INTEGRATION_KEY"
        description: "{{ .CommonAnnotations.summary }}"

  - name: auto-kill-switch
    webhook_configs:
      - url: "http://agent-control-plane:8080/api/v1/kill-switch"
        http_config:
          bearer_token: "${KILL_SWITCH_TOKEN}"

Consider a production research agent fleet that processes incoming document analysis requests. The fleet runs twenty concurrent agent workers, each capable of making multiple LLM calls per task. Tasks arrive via a message queue and can take anywhere from 5 seconds (short document, fast model) to 5 minutes (long document, multi-step reasoning). The fleet operates with a hard budget of $50 per hour across all workers.

The alerting setup has three tiers. The first tier uses the Prometheus instrumentation shown above, with an additional metric for queue depth (tasks waiting) and a histogram for the number of reasoning iterations per run. The second tier evaluates seven alert rules in Prometheus covering cost, latency, error rate, queue saturation, and stuck agents. The third tier routes warnings to a Slack channel monitored by the engineering team and critical alerts to PagerDuty, with a separate webhook receiver for the budget overrun case that calls an internal kill-switch API.

# Real-world: agent with iteration-count alerting and kill switch support
# pip install prometheus_client anthropic redis

import anthropic
import redis
import time
from prometheus_client import Counter, Histogram, Gauge, start_http_server

# Additional metrics for iteration depth
agent_iterations = Histogram(
    "agent_iterations_per_run",
    "Number of reasoning iterations (LLM turns) per completed run",
    ["agent_type"],
    buckets=[1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 12, 20, 30],
)
queue_depth = Gauge(
    "agent_queue_depth",
    "Number of tasks waiting in the input queue",
    ["agent_type"],
)

KILL_SWITCH_KEY = "agent:kill_switch"  # Redis key set by the control-plane webhook


class ResearchAgent:
    MAX_ITERATIONS = 15  # hard ceiling per run

    def __init__(self, client: anthropic.Anthropic, redis_client: redis.Redis):
        self.client = client
        self.redis = redis_client

    def _is_killed(self) -> bool:
        """Check Redis kill switch set by Alertmanager webhook on budget overrun."""
        return self.redis.exists(KILL_SWITCH_KEY) == 1

    def run(self, task: str, agent_type: str = "research") -> str:
        if self._is_killed():
            raise RuntimeError("Agent fleet suspended: budget kill switch is active")

        messages = [{"role": "user", "content": task}]
        tools = build_tool_definitions()  # your tool schemas
        iterations = 0
        agent_runs_active.labels(agent_type=agent_type).inc()

        try:
            while iterations < self.MAX_ITERATIONS:
                if self._is_killed():
                    raise RuntimeError("Kill switch activated mid-run")

                response = call_llm_with_metrics(
                    self.client, messages, agent_type=agent_type
                )
                iterations += 1

                if response.stop_reason == "end_turn":
                    # Extract final text answer
                    for block in response.content:
                        if hasattr(block, "text"):
                            return block.text
                    return ""

                if response.stop_reason == "tool_use":
                    tool_results = dispatch_tools(response.content, tools)
                    messages = messages + [
                        {"role": "assistant", "content": response.content},
                        {"role": "user", "content": tool_results},
                    ]

            # Exceeded MAX_ITERATIONS — log and return partial
            agent_run_errors_total.labels(
                agent_type=agent_type, error_class="MaxIterationsExceeded"
            ).inc()
            return "[truncated: max iterations reached]"

        finally:
            agent_iterations.labels(agent_type=agent_type).observe(iterations)
            agent_runs_active.labels(agent_type=agent_type).dec()


def run_worker_loop(agent: ResearchAgent, queue_name: str = "research:tasks"):
    """Pulls tasks from Redis queue and runs the agent."""
    r = agent.redis
    while True:
        queue_depth.labels(agent_type="research").set(r.llen(queue_name))
        item = r.blpop(queue_name, timeout=5)  # blocking pop, 5s timeout
        if item is None:
            continue  # heartbeat iteration
        _, task_json = item
        task = task_json.decode()
        try:
            result = agent.run(task)
            r.rpush("research:results", result)
        except RuntimeError as exc:
            if "kill switch" in str(exc):
                # Stop the worker loop on kill switch; operator must reset
                raise
            r.rpush("research:errors", str(exc))
In production, each of the twenty workers runs this loop in a separate process. Prometheus scrapes all twenty metrics endpoints (on ports 9090–9109) and aggregates them. The

histogram reveals within 30 minutes if a new prompt causes agents to spiral into deep reasoning loops — a pattern invisible from cost or latency metrics alone because individual LLM calls remain fast but the total run count explodes.
Alerting on the Mean Instead of Percentiles

means the alert fires only when the majority of calls are slow. A system where 95% of calls finish in 5 seconds but 5% hang for 120 seconds will have a mean around 11 seconds — the 5% tail is invisible. Use
to expose the experience of the slowest users.
No Dead Man's Switch for Stuck Agents

A stuck agent that is waiting on a deadlocked tool call produces zero metric updates — counters stop incrementing, histograms stop receiving observations. An alert that checks for a metric crossing a threshold will never fire because the metric is static, not rising. The correct pattern is to alert on the absence of expected activity: if
rule in the example above implements this pattern.
as a metric label creates a new time series for every agent run, rapidly exhausting Prometheus memory. Labels should be low-cardinality dimensions like
. Use distributed tracing (with run IDs as trace attributes, not metric labels) for per-run detail.
Alerting on Cumulative Counters Without rate()
Using a raw counter value in an alert expression —
— fires permanently once the threshold is crossed, even if the error rate has dropped to zero. Always use
alerts on more than 0.5 errors per second over the last 5 minutes — a rate that correctly auto-resolves when errors stop.
An alert that fires to a PagerDuty integration that has an expired API key will silently fail. Test every receiver configuration by injecting a synthetic alert via
and verifying end-to-end delivery. Include receiver health checks in regular operational reviews.

A rising error rate is a symptom that affects users. The cause might be a model API outage, a bad prompt, or a broken tool. Alert on the symptom (error rate) and let the engineer diagnose the cause from the dashboard.

Define at least three: informational (dashboard only), warning (Slack notification, no page), critical (PagerDuty page). This prevents alert fatigue while ensuring severe incidents receive immediate human attention.

For any agent fleet that is expected to always be processing tasks, alert if throughput drops to zero while the fleet is supposedly running. This catches scheduler bugs, network partitions, and complete process crashes that no other alert detects.
Track token cost as a first-class metric.

Record input and output token counts separately on every LLM call. Output tokens cost significantly more than input tokens for most models. Tracking them separately lets alerts distinguish a cost spike caused by large input context (often benign) from one caused by runaway output generation (often pathological).

Every alert should have a linked runbook explaining what the alert means, what to investigate first, and what actions to take. An on-call engineer receiving a PagerDuty page at 3 AM must not need to reverse-engineer the alert semantics before they can act.
A batch summarization agent that normally consumes 50K tokens per run has a very different cost profile from a real-time Q&A agent consuming 2K tokens. Use
labels and group-by clauses to apply different thresholds per workload type.
Alert on queue depth, not just throughput.

A queue that is growing faster than it is being consumed indicates either a burst in demand or a slowdown in processing. Catching this early allows horizontal scaling before the queue overflows and tasks are dropped.

Track the alert-to-action ratio: how often does an alert fire and an engineer takes meaningful action versus closes the page without action. Alerts with a low action ratio are candidates for threshold adjustment or removal.
Prometheus's scrape model scales well for agent fleets up to a few hundred worker processes. Each worker exposes a

endpoint; Prometheus scrapes each endpoint every 15 seconds. At 200 workers, that is roughly 13 scrapes per second — well within Prometheus's capacity. Each metric sample consumes approximately 2 bytes in Prometheus's compressed time-series store, so a worker emitting 50 distinct metric series over 30 days uses about 200 MB of storage.

At larger scales (thousands of agent workers), the scrape model becomes bottlenecked. The correct pattern is to switch from pull-based scraping to push-based collection using a Prometheus Pushgateway or an OTEL Collector with a Prometheus remote-write exporter. The OTEL Collector aggregates metrics from all workers before pushing to Prometheus, dramatically reducing the number of scrape targets and the storage cost.

The primary scalability risk is cardinality explosion. Each unique combination of label values creates a new time series. Keep label sets to three or four dimensions with low cardinality (fewer than 20 values each). Avoid agent instance ID, user ID, or task ID as labels.

If an agent loop runs thousands of iterations per minute, recording a histogram observation on every iteration is expensive. Use a reservoir sampling approach: record every Nth iteration (N=10 or N=100), or record the aggregate (total iterations for the run) rather than each iteration as it happens.
Complex PromQL expressions across large time windows (e.g.,
over thousands of series) are computationally expensive. Pre-compute expensive sub-expressions as recording rules so alert expressions query pre-aggregated data rather than raw samples.

Default Prometheus retention is 15 days. For long-term trend analysis and alerting-history audits, configure remote storage (Thanos, VictoriaMetrics, or Cortex) to retain data for 90 days or longer, while keeping local Prometheus retention short for performance.
Time-series storage, PromQL rules, Alertmanager integration
Open source, mature, huge ecosystem, pull-based simplicity
Dashboards, stat tiles, alert state panels, multi-datasource
Rich UI, Grafana Alerting subsystem, integrates with Prometheus and Datadog
Metrics, traces, logs, LLM Observability add-on
SaaS, zero infrastructure, native LLM cost tracking, anomaly detection
Trace-level token cost, latency, user sessions for LLM apps
Purpose-built for LLMs, cost per trace, quality scores, open-source self-hostable
Run tracing, evals, latency, token usage for LangChain agents
Deep LangChain integration, eval datasets, feedback capture
Vendor-neutral spans, metrics, and logs; OTLP protocol
Write once, export to any backend; CNCF standard, broad language support
On-call scheduling, escalation policies, acknowledgement tracking
Proven incident routing, mobile apps, SLA timer integration

Metrics-based alerting is the right primary approach for cost, latency, and quality signals because it provides low detection latency and supports sophisticated PromQL expressions like absence-of-activity detection. Log-based alerting is a good supplement for detecting specific error strings or exception types. APM / tracing tools (Datadog, Honeycomb) add rich per-trace context that complements fleet-level metrics. Synthetic probing alone is insufficient for agents because it cannot detect cost runaway or silent quality degradation.

The alerting pipeline is itself an operational dependency and must be treated with the same reliability care as the agents it monitors. An Alertmanager that is down during an incident prevents notifications from reaching on-call engineers — the agent fleet may be burning budget with no human aware of it. Run Alertmanager in a high-availability configuration with at least two replicas using gossip clustering for deduplication. Monitor Prometheus itself with an external uptime check (a separate Blackbox Exporter or a cloud uptime service).

Auto-remediation webhook receivers must require authentication (bearer token, HMAC signature, or mTLS). An unauthenticated kill-switch endpoint is a denial-of-service vector — an attacker who can send a POST to it can halt the entire agent fleet.

PagerDuty routing keys, Slack webhook URLs, and email relay credentials stored in Alertmanager configuration are sensitive. Store them in a secrets manager (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) and inject them as environment variables. Do not commit them to version control.

Alert rule files committed to a Git repository provide an audit trail. Any change to a threshold requires a code review. This prevents accidental (or malicious) silencing of critical alerts by raising the threshold above the incident level.

Run a monthly "fire drill" by manually triggering a non-critical synthetic alert through the full routing pipeline. Verify it arrives at the Slack channel and the PagerDuty on-call schedule. This catches expired integration keys and misconfigured routes before a real incident.
During a large incident (e.g., an API outage affecting all agent types simultaneously), hundreds of alerts may fire at once. Alertmanager's
reduce notification storms: a critical alert for
can inhibit warning-level cost alerts for the same

Kill switches and circuit breakers invoked by alert webhooks are powerful but dangerous. Implement a confirmation step: the webhook sets a flag in a shared store, and agent processes poll the flag on each iteration rather than being killed externally. This avoids corrupting in-flight runs and gives the agent a chance to finish cleanly.

Instrument every LLM call and tool invocation with counters and histograms; expose the metrics endpoint for Prometheus scraping — this is the minimum viable alerting foundation for any production agent.
Track token cost with separate input and output counters, then compute estimated hourly spend in alert rules; this is the most important signal for preventing budget overruns.
Alert on percentiles (p95, p99), not averages — averages mask the tail latency that degrades user experience and hides runaway long-running tasks.

Implement a dead man's switch: alert if active runs exist but no LLM calls have been made in N minutes; this is the only reliable way to detect stuck or deadlocked agents whose metrics have gone silent.

Use graduated severity tiers (warning to Slack, critical to PagerDuty, budget overrun to auto-remediation webhook) to avoid alert fatigue while ensuring severe incidents receive immediate human attention.
Keep metric label cardinality low — use agent type, model, and status as labels, never run ID or user ID; use distributed tracing for per-run detail, not metrics.

Treat the alerting pipeline itself as a reliability-critical dependency: run Alertmanager in HA mode, test delivery end-to-end monthly, secure webhook endpoints with authentication, and store credentials in a secrets manager.
